content.text: 22 sessions, no existing agents yet, and the dogfood case study lives at `course/capstone/case-study.md`. Next requirement number is **R18**. Let me read the structural precedents: the existing case study, the post-v1 enhancement playbook, and the EARS style of the recent R14/R16/R17 requirements.

content.content:
1	# Case study: how this course was built and is maintained with Claude Code
2	
3	This is the **worked exemplar** for your capstone — and it is real. This course was itself
4	built end-to-end with Claude Code, spec-driven, against the same discipline the units teach. Read it
5	before you start your capstone so you have a concrete picture of a non-trivial job done well: context
6	engineered deliberately, custom extensions built and used, a real workflow followed, and Claude's
7	output verified at every step rather than trusted.
8	
9	It is also the course's standing dogfooding record: if we teach a feature, we point at the
10	real artifact we used, never a prop.
11	
12	## 1. The job
13	
14	Take a seasoned developer who is new to AI-assisted coding to elite Claude Code practitioner — as a
15	self-paced, hands-on course organized around real use cases, tiered for fast learners, and resilient
16	to a fast-moving CLI. That is a large, ambiguous brief with no single right answer, exactly the kind
17	of work where ad-hoc prompting falls apart and a deliberate process pays for itself.
18	
19	## 2. The workflow: spec-driven, with real gates
20	
21	We ran the [Kiro-style spec workflow](../units/10-spec-driven-dev/unit.md) the course teaches,
22	on the course itself. The artifacts are not illustrations — they are the spec we actually built from:
23	
24	- **Requirements** ([`requirements.md`](../../specs/claude-code-mastery/requirements.md)) — R1–R17 in
25	  EARS form, with stable IDs. Approved turn-by-turn **before** any design work began.
26	- **Design** ([`design.md`](../../specs/claude-code-mastery/design.md)) — architecture, the unit
27	  model, the version-resilience mechanism, the lab/codebase plan, and a traceability table mapping
28	  every requirement to the component that satisfies it. Approved **before** tasks.
29	- **Tasks** ([`tasks.md`](../../specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks.md)) — the ordered build plan, chunked
30	  into phases (scaffold → tooling → codebases → units → finalize) so each could be executed in a
31	  bounded context window.
32	
33	The gates were genuine, not ceremony: requirements were locked before design, design before tasks. A
34	held gate is the difference between a spec and a wish — when a requirement felt arbitrary later, the
35	reasoning was already recorded rather than re-litigated. That "why" lives in
36	[`decisions.md`](../../specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md), the build's decision log,
37	which a fresh session reads to recover context it never had.
38	
39	**Context was engineered, not dumped.** Each unit was authored in its own slice, loading only the
40	relevant requirements, the one design section, and the cross-cutting `meta/` artifacts it referenced —
41	never the whole spec. Project memory ([`CLAUDE.md`](../../CLAUDE.md), the **Memory & context** example) carries the
42	working agreements into every session so they did not have to be re-stated.
43	
44	## 3. The custom extensions we built and use
45	
46	These are authentic — each one earns its place by doing a job in the real build loop:
47	
48	| Artifact | What it does | Taught in |
49	|---|---|---|
50	| [`prime-context` skill](../../.claude/skills/prime-context/SKILL.md) | Loads full project context at the start of a fresh session (read order + live status + open loops). | [Commands & skills](../units/12-commands-and-skills/unit.md) |
51	| [`close-unit` command](../../.claude/commands/close-unit.md) | Runs the post-authoring state-sync chore (update status, check off tasks, log the decision, run the checks). | [Commands & skills](../units/12-commands-and-skills/unit.md) |
52	| [Enforcement hook](../../.claude/settings.json) + check suite | A `PostToolUse` hook runs `make check` on edits under `course/`/`meta/` and blocks in-session on failure. | [Hooks](../units/14-hooks/unit.md) |
53	| [`tools/check-version-drift`](../../tools/check-version-drift) | Flags when the installed CLI has drifted from the verified record (the refresh trigger). | [Automate & scale](../units/16-automate-and-scale/unit.md) |
54	| [MCP server + config](../../codebases/fixtures/taskflow_mcp.py) | A real, connectable local stdio MCP server over taskflow data, plus its project config. | [MCP & vetting](../units/15-mcp-and-vetting/unit.md) |
55	| [`tools/doctor`](../../tools/doctor) | Preflight: install, version floor, auth, a first command. | [Onboarding](../units/01-onboarding-first-win/unit.md) |
56	| [CI workflow](../../.github/workflows/checks.yml) | Runs the full check suite on every push (the backstop gate). | [Automate & scale](../units/16-automate-and-scale/unit.md) |
57	
58	`prime-context` and `close-unit` **bookend the real authoring loop**: prime at session start, close
59	the unit when its prose is done. That is the same shape your capstone should take — package the work
60	you repeat into a command or skill rather than retyping it.
61	
62	## 4. Verification was the through-line
63	
64	The thesis the whole course defends — verify, don't trust — is what kept the build honest:
65	
66	- **Never author a version-specific value from memory.** Every command, flag, and settings key was
67	  verified against the installed CLI and quarantined into
68	  [`meta/version-data.yaml`](../../meta/version-data.yaml), referenced by key. When verification
69	  contradicted memory, verification won (the `--permission-mode` value set turned out broader than the
70	  first draft assumed). Keys that could not be confirmed headlessly were marked `unverified` rather
71	  than faked.
72	- **Labs verify behavior, not wording.** Each automated `verify.sh` asserts the contract (and that the
73	  full suite stays green), so any convention-respecting solution passes and a plausible-but-wrong one
74	  fails — the same standard you should hold Claude's output to.
75	- **The checks gate the build.** Front-matter, coverage, link, version-reference, and traceability
76	  checks run at three layers (in-session hook, git pre-commit, CI). `make check-strict` going green is
77	  the single mechanical signal that the Definition of Done's structural obligations are met.
78	
79	## 5. Maintenance: keeping it current as the CLI changes
80	
81	The course is built to be refreshed, not rewritten, when Claude Code moves. The process — run
82	it whenever the CLI updates — is:
83	
84	1. Run [`tools/check-version-drift`](../../tools/check-version-drift) to compare the installed
85	   `claude --version` against [`meta/version-record.md`](../../meta/version-record.md) and surface
86	   new/removed commands.
87	2. Re-verify each flagged version-specific detail against the installed CLI (`--help`, in-REPL
88	   `/help`, docs) — never from memory.
89	3. Update **only** [`meta/version-data.yaml`](../../meta/version-data.yaml) (and the rare prose it
90	   genuinely affects); because version-specifics are referenced by key rather than inlined, a version
91	   bump touches a bounded set of files, not the prose.
92	4. Bump [`meta/version-record.md`](../../meta/version-record.md) with the new verified version and date.
93	5. Re-run the enforcement suite (`make check`) until green.
94	
95	The [maintainer guide](../maintainer-guide.md) covers adding or updating a unit without breaking the
96	catalog/matrix/map.

# Case study: how this course was built and is maintained with Claude Code

This is the **worked exemplar** for your capstone — and it is real. This course was itself
built end-to-end with Claude Code, spec-driven, against the same discipline the units teach. Read it
before you start your capstone so you have a concrete picture of a non-trivial job done well: context
engineered deliberately, custom extensions built and used, a real workflow followed, and Claude's
output verified at every step rather than trusted.

It is also the course's standing dogfooding record: if we teach a feature, we point at the
real artifact we used, never a prop.

## 1. The job

Take a seasoned developer who is new to AI-assisted coding to elite Claude Code practitioner — as a
self-paced, hands-on course organized around real use cases, tiered for fast learners, and resilient
to a fast-moving CLI. That is a large, ambiguous brief with no single right answer, exactly the kind
of work where ad-hoc prompting falls apart and a deliberate process pays for itself.

## 2. The workflow: spec-driven, with real gates

We ran the [Kiro-style spec workflow](../units/10-spec-driven-dev/unit.md) the course teaches,
on the course itself. The artifacts are not illustrations — they are the spec we actually built from:

- **Requirements** ([`requirements.md`](../../specs/claude-code-mastery/requirements.md)) — R1–R17 in
  EARS form, with stable IDs. Approved turn-by-turn **before** any design work began.
- **Design** ([`design.md`](../../specs/claude-code-mastery/design.md)) — architecture, the unit
  model, the version-resilience mechanism, the lab/codebase plan, and a traceability table mapping
  every requirement to the component that satisfies it. Approved **before** tasks.
- **Tasks** ([`tasks.md`](../../specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks.md)) — the ordered build plan, chunked
  into phases (scaffold → tooling → codebases → units → finalize) so each could be executed in a
  bounded context window.

The gates were genuine, not ceremony: requirements were locked before design, design before tasks. A
held gate is the difference between a spec and a wish — when a requirement felt arbitrary later, the
reasoning was already recorded rather than re-litigated. That "why" lives in
[`decisions.md`](../../specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md), the build's decision log,
which a fresh session reads to recover context it never had.

**Context was engineered, not dumped.** Each unit was authored in its own slice, loading only the
relevant requirements, the one design section, and the cross-cutting `meta/` artifacts it referenced —
never the whole spec. Project memory ([`CLAUDE.md`](../../CLAUDE.md), the **Memory & context** example) carries the
working agreements into every session so they did not have to be re-stated.

## 3. The custom extensions we built and use

These are authentic — each one earns its place by doing a job in the real build loop:

| Artifact | What it does | Taught in |
|---|---|---|
| [`prime-context` skill](../../.claude/skills/prime-context/SKILL.md) | Loads full project context at the start of a fresh session (read order + live status + open loops). | [Commands & skills](../units/12-commands-and-skills/unit.md) |
| [`close-unit` command](../../.claude/commands/close-unit.md) | Runs the post-authoring state-sync chore (update status, check off tasks, log the decision, run the checks). | [Commands & skills](../units/12-commands-and-skills/unit.md) |
| [Enforcement hook](../../.claude/settings.json) + check suite | A `PostToolUse` hook runs `make check` on edits under `course/`/`meta/` and blocks in-session on failure. | [Hooks](../units/14-hooks/unit.md) |
| [`tools/check-version-drift`](../../tools/check-version-drift) | Flags when the installed CLI has drifted from the verified record (the refresh trigger). | [Automate & scale](../units/16-automate-and-scale/unit.md) |
| [MCP server + config](../../codebases/fixtures/taskflow_mcp.py) | A real, connectable local stdio MCP server over taskflow data, plus its project config. | [MCP & vetting](../units/15-mcp-and-vetting/unit.md) |
| [`tools/doctor`](../../tools/doctor) | Preflight: install, version floor, auth, a first command. | [Onboarding](../units/01-onboarding-first-win/unit.md) |
| [CI workflow](../../.github/workflows/checks.yml) | Runs the full check suite on every push (the backstop gate). | [Automate & scale](../units/16-automate-and-scale/unit.md) |

`prime-context` and `close-unit` **bookend the real authoring loop**: prime at session start, close
the unit when its prose is done. That is the same shape your capstone should take — package the work
you repeat into a command or skill rather than retyping it.

## 4. Verification was the through-line

The thesis the whole course defends — verify, don't trust — is what kept the build honest:

- **Never author a version-specific value from memory.** Every command, flag, and settings key was
  verified against the installed CLI and quarantined into
  [`meta/version-data.yaml`](../../meta/version-data.yaml), referenced by key. When verification
  contradicted memory, verification won (the `--permission-mode` value set turned out broader than the
  first draft assumed). Keys that could not be confirmed headlessly were marked `unverified` rather
  than faked.
- **Labs verify behavior, not wording.** Each automated `verify.sh` asserts the contract (and that the
  full suite stays green), so any convention-respecting solution passes and a plausible-but-wrong one
  fails — the same standard you should hold Claude's output to.
- **The checks gate the build.** Front-matter, coverage, link, version-reference, and traceability
  checks run at three layers (in-session hook, git pre-commit, CI). `make check-strict` going green is
  the single mechanical signal that the Definition of Done's structural obligations are met.

## 5. Maintenance: keeping it current as the CLI changes

The course is built to be refreshed, not rewritten, when Claude Code moves. The process — run
it whenever the CLI updates — is:

1. Run [`tools/check-version-drift`](../../tools/check-version-drift) to compare the installed
   `claude --version` against [`meta/version-record.md`](../../meta/version-record.md) and surface
   new/removed commands.
2. Re-verify each flagged version-specific detail against the installed CLI (`--help`, in-REPL
   `/help`, docs) — never from memory.
3. Update **only** [`meta/version-data.yaml`](../../meta/version-data.yaml) (and the rare prose it
   genuinely affects); because version-specifics are referenced by key rather than inlined, a version
   bump touches a bounded set of files, not the prose.
4. Bump [`meta/version-record.md`](../../meta/version-record.md) with the new verified version and date.
5. Re-run the enforcement suite (`make check`) until green.

The [maintainer guide](../maintainer-guide.md) covers adding or updating a unit without breaking the
catalog/matrix/map.

## 6. AI-authorship transparency

This course — its units, labs, spec, and tooling — was authored collaboratively with Claude Code,
under human direction and review at every gate. That is the point, not a caveat: the course teaches a
way of working, and it was built that way.

In the spirit of the responsible-output guidance the course itself teaches: AI-assisted
authorship does not lower the bar for correctness or attribution. Everything here was reviewed by a
human, version-specific claims were verified against the real tool rather than asserted from a model's
memory, and the reasoning behind non-obvious choices was recorded so it can be checked rather than
trusted. When you ship work produced with Claude — including your capstone — hold it to that same
standard: you are accountable for the output, so verify it and disclose the assistance honestly.

217	### R13 — Maintainability of the course
218	**Acceptance criteria**
219	- R13.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL keep a consistent, documented directory and file-naming convention.
220	- R13.AC2 — THE COURSE SHALL centralize cross-cutting facts (version record, glossary, conventions, use-case catalog, capability map, coverage matrix) so each is defined once and referenced, not duplicated, and SHALL store each such artifact in a **machine-readable** form so the checks in R13.AC4/AC5 can read it (consistent with R6.AC3 and R12.AC2).
221	- R13.AC3 — THE COURSE SHALL include a contributor/maintainer guide describing how to add or update a unit without breaking the use-case catalog, coverage matrix, or capability map; the guide SHALL include a recipe for **authoring a unit with Claude Code** using the course's own authoring command/skill (R14).
222	- R13.AC4 — THE COURSE SHALL provide a **required minimal enforcement suite** of automated checks: (a) unit front-matter schema validation (R6.AC3); (b) coverage/capability-map cross-validation — every capability area (R4) and every can-do statement (R1) is actually covered; (c) link checking; (d) version-data reference integrity — every prose reference resolves to a key in the version-data file (R12.AC2). Additional checks MAY be added where practical.
223	- R13.AC5 — THE COURSE SHALL provide **automated traceability checks** asserting that every requirement ID is referenced by at least one unit/artifact and that every can-do statement traces to at least one lab and at least one capstone-rubric dimension (automating §8).
224	- R13.AC6 — THE COURSE SHALL run the checks in R13.AC4/AC5 both as **local hooks** (fast authoring feedback) and in **CI** (backstop gate); these checks SHALL themselves serve as worked examples of hooks/CI (R14).
225	
226	### R14 — Self-demonstrating ("dogfooding")
227	**Acceptance criteria**
228	- R14.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL itself be a worked artifact of the practices it teaches; at minimum this spec, a `CLAUDE.md`, and at least one course-authoring custom command/skill SHALL exist and be referenced as living examples.
229	- R14.AC2 — WHERE the course teaches a feature for which a genuine artifact exists in this repository (e.g., the enforcement hooks of R13, the drift check of R12, an authoring subagent/skill, an MCP configuration), THE COURSE SHALL reference that real artifact as the worked example ("if we built the course with it, show it").
230	- R14.AC3 — THE COURSE SHALL use only **authentic** self-examples — artifacts genuinely used to build or maintain the course — and SHALL NOT fabricate contrived "prop" artifacts solely to have an example (consistent with R2.AC4).
231	- R14.AC4 — THE COURSE SHALL include a "how this course was built and is maintained with Claude Code" **case study**, covering the spec-driven workflow and the update process (R12.AC7); this case study SHALL be cross-referenced as exemplar material for the capstone (R8.AC2).
232	- R14.AC5 — THE COURSE SHALL include a light, honest **AI-authorship transparency note** disclosing that parts were authored with Claude Code, modeling the responsible-output guidance of R10.AC9.
233	- R14.AC6 — WHERE the course references its own artifacts as examples, THE COURSE SHALL keep those references accurate to the repository's actual contents (enforced via R13.AC4).
234	- R14.AC7 — THE COURSE MAY maintain a **decision/build log** (e.g., the evolution of this spec and key decisions) as an aid to maintainers and optional teaching material.
235	
236	### R15 — Accessibility & portability
237	**Acceptance criteria**
238	- R15.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL be readable as plain Markdown in any standard viewer with no proprietary tooling required (any generated site is optional and derived from the Markdown source).
239	- R15.AC2 — THE COURSE SHALL author content in **CommonMark-compatible** Markdown for its core meaning; richer rendering features (e.g., GitHub callouts, Mermaid diagrams) MAY be used only as enhancement and SHALL degrade gracefully so comprehension never depends on them.
240	- R15.AC3 — THE COURSE SHALL keep labs runnable on macOS and Linux, with **WSL as the supported Windows story for v1**; native Windows (PowerShell/cmd) is documented as out-of-scope for v1 rather than silently unsupported. Platform-specific divergence SHALL be noted.
241	- R15.AC4 — THE COURSE SHALL author labs **portable-by-default**, preferring cross-platform commands/scripts and isolating any unavoidable platform-specific steps into clearly-marked sections (mirroring the quarantine pattern of R12.AC2).
242	- R15.AC5 — THE COURSE SHALL avoid hard dependencies on paid third-party services for any required (non-optional) lab. Claude Code access itself is the assumed course prerequisite (A1) and is exempt from this rule; this AC governs *additional, incidental* paid dependencies — no required lab SHALL depend on a second paid service.
243	- R15.AC6 — THE COURSE SHALL meet baseline **accessibility**: diagrams and images SHALL have text equivalents; meaning SHALL NOT be conveyed by color or emoji alone; and document structure (headings, lists) SHALL be used semantically for navigability.
244	
245	### R16 — Exhaustive, generated CLI reference (version-resilient)
246	**User story:** As a learner (and the course maintainer), I want an exhaustive, always-current reference of the Claude Code CLI surface generated directly from the installed tool, so that I have an authoritative options reference that never drifts from the actual CLI and that the course's own tooling can rely on. *(Added post-v1, 2026-05-31; anchors additively to R12/R13/R14/R5–R6.)*
247	
248	**Acceptance criteria**
249	- R16.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL maintain an **exhaustive, machine-readable CLI reference** (every command, subcommand, and flag with its description) generated by recursively introspecting the installed CLI (`claude --help` over subcommands), stored as a single committed source-of-truth artifact in `meta/`.
250	- R16.AC2 — THE COURSE SHALL render a **human-readable, learner-facing** reference (committed) **from the machine artifact alone** — never re-introspecting or hand-restating values — following the committed-rendered generation pattern (R12.AC2), drift-gated by the enforcement suite (R13.AC4).
251	- R16.AC3 — Every reference entry SHALL carry **verification provenance** (R12.AC4): live `--help` output for introspected entries, or a documentation URL + retrieval date for entries sourced from the curated supplement. No entry SHALL be authored from model memory (R12.AC3).
252	- R16.AC4 — WHERE the CLI surface includes capabilities not exposed by `claude --help` (e.g., in-REPL slash commands), THE COURSE MAY enrich the reference from a **single curated supplement file** carrying its own provenance; the generator SHALL union introspected and supplement entries deterministically and mark each entry's source.
253	- R16.AC5 — WHEN version drift is detected (R12.AC6), the refresh process (R12.AC7) SHALL **regenerate the machine reference from the installed CLI and re-render the human page** — bootstrapped by the drift trigger rather than maintained by hand — touching only the generated artifacts (R12.AC8).
254	- R16.AC6 — Generation and rendering SHALL be **deterministic and offline-safe for CI**: given the same installed CLI and supplement, output SHALL be byte-stable, and the rendered artifact's freshness SHALL be verifiable by a regenerate-and-diff check in the enforcement suite (R13.AC4).
255	- R16.AC7 — THE COURSE SHALL **dogfood** this feature as a worked example (R14.AC1/AC2): the spec-driven build of the reference SHALL be referenced from **U10** (spec-driven development) and the artifact itself from **U4** (memory & context / single-source version data) as living examples; references SHALL stay accurate (R14.AC6, enforced by R13.AC4).
256	
257	### R17 — CLI version-change synopsis on refresh (changelog digest)
258	**User story:** As the course maintainer, when I bump the course to a newer Claude Code CLI, I want a recorded synopsis of what changed — drawn from the official online release notes and spanning every version between the last one the repo tracked and the new one — so the maintenance trail shows what moved and any course content affected by those changes is surfaced for review. *(Added post-v1, 2026-05-31; extends the R12 refresh process; composes with R16.)*
259	
260	**Acceptance criteria**
261	- R17.AC1 — WHEN the course is refreshed to a new CLI version (R12.AC7), THE COURSE SHALL consult the **official online Claude Code release notes/changelog** and record a **synopsis of the changes** as a committed, maintainer-facing artifact in `meta/` (a running per-version digest).
262	- R17.AC2 — The synopsis SHALL be **cumulative across the gap**: covering **all versions strictly after the last recorded verified version up to and including the new one** (the repo may skip intermediate releases between refreshes) — not only the newest point release.
263	- R17.AC3 — Each entry SHALL carry **verification provenance** (R12.AC4): the changelog/release-notes URL and retrieval date. No change SHALL be summarized from model memory (R12.AC3); an unreachable source SHALL be **marked, not fabricated**.
264	- R17.AC4 — The synopsis SHALL **flag changes that may affect course content** — new/removed/renamed commands or flags, changed defaults, deprecations — cross-referenced to the affected `{{vd:key}}` values and the regenerated CLI reference (R16), bounding the refresh review surface (R12.AC8).
265	- R17.AC5 — THE COURSE SHALL provide an **automated check** that the recorded verified version (R12.AC5) has a **matching synopsis entry**, so a version bump lacking its digest is surfaced by the enforcement suite (R13.AC4) rather than left to memory (R12.AC6 philosophy).
266	- R17.AC6 — The documented refresh process (R12.AC7) and the post-v1 enhancement playbook (R13.AC3) SHALL include this synopsis step, making it a standing part of every version bump.
267	
268	---
269	
270	## 5. Assumptions & dependencies
271	- A1 — The learner can install and authenticate a recent Claude Code CLI independently of this course (the course verifies, not provisions).
272	- A2 — The learner has a working terminal, Git, and a code editor, and is a proficient software engineer.
273	- A3 — Both sample codebases' language/stack (primary + legacy) will be selected in Design (candidate: a small, widely-readable stack to minimize language-specific friction); see R7.AC2.
274	- A4 — Some capabilities (e.g., enterprise managed settings) may be demonstrated conceptually where a learner lacks the environment to run them; such cases fall under R4.AC4/R4.AC5.
275	
276	## 6. Out of scope (v1)

## Adding a post-v1 enhancement (a new requirement)

A *refresh* (above) keeps the same scope against a newer CLI. Occasionally the course gains a new
**capability of its own** — a new generated artifact, check, or guarantee. Don't bolt it on ad hoc;
run it through the same spec-driven, gated path the course teaches and was built with. The exhaustive
CLI reference (R16) and the version-change digest (R17) were added exactly this way in **P8** — read
[`../specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks/P8-cli-reference.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/tasks/P8-cli-reference.md)
as the worked example. The additive pattern, each step gated per the root
[`CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md) working agreements:

1. **New requirement.** Add a `### Rn — …` heading with testable EARS acceptance criteria to
   [`requirements.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/requirements.md). **Approve it at the requirements
   gate before designing.** IDs are append-only — never renumber existing requirements; downstream
   artifacts cite them.
2. **Design it.** Amend [`design.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/design.md) with a section that serves
   the requirement, each choice tagged with the `[Rn]` it traces to. **Approve at the design gate before
   writing tasks.**
3. **Plan it.** Add an ordered `tasks/P{N}-<slug>.md` build plan (don't overload an existing phase
   file); execute in slices, `make check` green after each.
4. **Record the why.** Append a [`decisions.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/decisions.md) entry,
   update the **🔓 open-loops ledger** for any deferred work, and refresh
   [`IMPLEMENTATION.md`](../specs/claude-code-mastery/IMPLEMENTATION.md) §3 at the phase boundary. The
   next session reads these, not your memory.
5. **Enforce it — generalized, never hardcoded.** This is the load-bearing rule. `check-traceability`
   **discovers** the requirement set from the `### Rn` headings, so the new requirement is enforced
   (it must be referenced by a delivered artifact) with **zero edits to the check**. If the enhancement
   adds a generated artifact, wire its `--check` into `make check`/`check-strict` (and `make drift` if it
   depends on the CLI), mirroring [`render-cli-reference`](../tools/render-cli-reference). When you must
   enumerate something, prefer discovery from the source of truth over a literal range.

What stays **closed**, deliberately: the **can-do set** (`C1–C17 + CV`) is a fixed taxonomy from R1's
design. An enhancement extends the *requirement* set and the artifacts, not the learner-outcome
taxonomy — a change that needs a new can-do is a curriculum change (back to the design gate for
R1/R2), not a post-v1 enhancement.

## Why this repo runs with auto-memory off

The committed [`.claude/settings.json`](../.claude/settings.json) sets `"autoMemoryEnabled": false`,
so Claude Code's per-machine auto-memory does **not** load or write while you work in this repo. This
is deliberate and consistent with the project's first working agreement — *source of truth is the
files, not memory* ([`CLAUDE.md`](../CLAUDE.md)). Every durable fact about this build lives in a
version-controlled file (`CLAUDE.md`, the spec, `meta/*`, the 🔓 open-loops ledger), so it stays
uniform across machines and shows up in diffs; auto-memory is per-machine and uncommitted, which would

aiTitle: Build case study with spec-driven development evaluation

content.text:
Good — that's an important accuracy check. The transcripts were **not** all Opus 4.8: **21 of 22 sessions ran on `claude-opus-4-8`**, but the very first and most foundational one — `2026-05-29_1845-requirements-and-design-spec-creation` (where requirements *and* design were authored) — ran on **`claude-sonnet-4-6`**. So the evaluation should attribute the AI side per-session by the model that actually ran, rather than calling the whole thing "Opus 4.8." I'll bake that honesty into the spec.

## How I'll run this (spec-driven, the repo's own post-v1 playbook)

This is a textbook **post-v1 enhancement**, and the repo has a gated playbook for exactly this (the P8 CLI-reference work is the worked precedent). So the path is:

1. **Requirement `R18`** — new `### R18` with testable EARS acceptance criteria, anchored additively to **R14** (dogfooding) and **R10/R3** (verification, collaboration). Approve at the requirements gate *before* design. (IDs are append-only; the can-do taxonomy C1–C17+CV stays closed — this extends the *requirement* set, not the learner-outcome taxonomy.)
2. **Design section** — placement, the persona panel, the three-tier evaluation model, caching format, provenance/honesty rules. Approve at the design gate *before* tasks.
3. **`tasks/P9-collaboration-retrospective.md`** — ordered slices, `make check` green after each.
4. **Record the why** — `decisions.md` entry + 🔓 ledger + `IMPLEMENTATION.md` §3.
5. **Enforce generalized** — `check-traceability` auto-discovers `### R18`, so it's enforced with zero edits to the check.

Two nice dogfooding fits fall out: the personas become **real `.claude/agents/*.md` definitions** (the repo has *zero* agents today, yet U13 teaches subagents — so this is authentic R14 material), and the whole exercise is itself a spec-driven build (U10).

**My candidate persona panel** (subjective, honest, non-sycophantic; each judges *both* the human and Claude through its lens, grading did-well / did-ok / can-improve):

1. **Process & gate discipline** — planning before building, holding spec gates, keeping state current
2. **Context engineering** — memory/`CLAUDE.md`, priming, slicing, avoiding context dumps
3. **Verification rigor** — verify-don't-trust; reading diffs; the "no version facts from memory" discipline
4. **Human→Claude communication** — prompt clarity, scoping, decisiveness, correcting drift early
5. **Claude→human communication** — explaining tradeoffs, asking vs. assuming, honest failure reporting, no sycophancy
6. **Tool & capability fluency** — right CLI feature at the right time; missed opportunities
7. **Collaboration & autonomy calibration** — delegation, initiative vs. over-asking, the correction loop
8. **Safety & responsibility** — blast radius, approval discipline, untrusted-content handling

Before I draft R18, two decisions are genuinely yours and shape the requirement directly:

input.new_string:
- R17.AC6 — The documented refresh process (R12.AC7) and the post-v1 enhancement playbook (R13.AC3) SHALL include this synopsis step, making it a standing part of every version bump.

### R18 — Collaboration retrospective (multi-agent, self-evaluating dogfood)
**User story:** As a learner (and the course maintainer), I want an honest, multi-perspective retrospective of how this course was actually built with Claude Code — what the human author and Claude each did well, did okay on, and could improve, across both process and human-language communication — produced by a panel of subjective persona reviewers reading the real session transcripts, so that the course teaches effective human+Claude co-authorship from a candid worked example rather than an idealized one. *(Added post-v1, 2026-05-31; anchors additively to R14 (dogfooding), R10 (responsible & verified use), R3 (workflow methods), and R8 (capstone exemplar).)*

**Acceptance criteria**
- R18.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL produce a **collaboration retrospective** evaluating its own build, assessing **both parties** — the human author and Claude — along two explicit axes, **process/workflow** and **human-language communication**, with each finding graded on a three-level scale: **did well / did okay / could improve**.
- R18.AC2 — The retrospective SHALL be produced by a **panel of distinct persona reviewers**, each implemented as a **real, committed Claude Code subagent** (`.claude/agents/`) focused on a key element of effective co-authorship / effective CLI use; the panel SHALL be a genuine dogfooded artifact (R14.AC1–AC3), not a contrived prop, and SHALL be referenced as a worked example from **U13** (subagents) — for which the repo previously had no authentic subagent to show (decision P5-U13-example).
- R18.AC3 — Every reviewer SHALL render **subjective, candid judgments** regardless of its persona: it SHALL NOT flatter, soften to please, or discourage, and SHALL aim for accuracy and thoroughness. Each judgment SHALL be **grounded in cited transcript evidence** (session + locator) and SHALL NOT be fabricated or asserted from memory (consistent with R10.AC9 and the R12.AC3 verify-don't-assume discipline).
- R18.AC4 — The evaluation SHALL proceed in **three tiers**: (a) a **per-session × per-reviewer** evaluation that considers only that one session, persisted ("cached") as a committed artifact; (b) a **per-session synthesis** consolidating every reviewer's evaluation of that session; (c) a **global synthesis** across all sessions. Each higher tier SHALL be derived from — and traceable to — the tier beneath it.
- R18.AC5 — The retrospective SHALL **attribute the AI side accurately, per session**, to the model that actually produced it (verified from the transcript record, never assumed — e.g., the foundational requirements/design session ran on `claude-sonnet-4-6` while the remaining sessions ran on `claude-opus-4-8`), so that no finding misattributes behavior to a model that did not produce it (R10.AC9 honesty; R12.AC3 philosophy).
- R18.AC6 — The retrospective SHALL be delivered as **both** a learner-facing **case study** (in `course/`, cross-referenced as capstone-exemplar material per R8.AC2 and R14.AC4) **and** a first-class internal **evaluation corpus** (the cached per-session × per-reviewer artifacts plus the per-session and global syntheses), each maintained as a real, accurate deliverable.
- R18.AC7 — The build of this retrospective SHALL itself follow the **spec-driven, gated process** the course teaches (R3.AC2) via the post-v1 enhancement playbook (R13.AC3), and be referenced as a dogfooding example (R14.AC2); the requirement (this R18) SHALL be enforced by the existing dynamically-discovered traceability check (R13.AC5) with **no special-casing**.
- R18.AC8 — The retrospective SHALL keep its references to the source material **accurate to the repository's actual `log/transcripts/` contents** (R14.AC6, enforced by R13.AC4) and SHALL introduce **no new secrets**, relying on the capture workflow's existing secret-scan gate.

content.text:
**R18 is drafted** in `specs/claude-code-mastery/requirements.md` (working tree only — not committed). Here it is for the **requirements-gate review**:

> ### R18 — Collaboration retrospective (multi-agent, self-evaluating dogfood)
> **User story:** an honest, multi-perspective retrospective of how this course was actually built with Claude Code — what the human author and Claude each did well, did okay on, and could improve, across process and human-language communication — produced by a panel of subjective persona reviewers reading the real transcripts, so the course teaches effective co-authorship from a candid worked example. *(Anchors to R14, R10, R3, R8.)*
>
> - **AC1** — evaluates **both parties** on two axes (process/workflow, human-language communication), graded **did well / did okay / could improve**.
> - **AC2** — produced by a **panel of persona reviewers**, each a **real committed `.claude/agents/` subagent** (authentic dogfood, not a prop); referenced from **U13** — which previously had no genuine subagent to show.
> - **AC3** — reviewers are **subjective and candid**: no flattery, no discouragement; every judgment **grounded in cited transcript evidence**, never from memory.
> - **AC4** — **three tiers**: per-session×per-reviewer (cached) → per-session synthesis → global synthesis; each higher tier traceable to the one beneath.
> - **AC5** — **accurate per-session model attribution** (foundational session = `sonnet-4-6`, rest = `opus-4-8`); no misattribution.
> - **AC6** — delivered as **both** a learner-facing case study (`course/`) **and** an internal evaluation corpus (`log/`).
> - **AC7** — built via the **spec-driven post-v1 playbook**; enforced by the existing dynamically-discovered traceability check, no special-casing.
> - **AC8** — references stay accurate to actual `log/transcripts/`; introduces no new secrets (relies on the existing scan gate).

A few notes on the deliberate choices:
- I **didn't add a new can-do** — the C1–C17+CV taxonomy is closed by design; this extends the *requirement* set only (per the playbook).
- AC2 turns this into a real authenticity win: the repo has **zero `.claude/agents/`** today even though U13 teaches subagents (decision P5-U13-example deliberately used an *illustrative* agent for lack of a genuine consumer). This panel becomes that genuine consumer.
- I haven't run `make check-strict` — it would (correctly) flag R18 as unreferenced until design/artifacts land. That resolves itself through the build, per the playbook.

**This is the requirements gate.** Please review R18. Once you approve (and tell me whether to commit it), I'll move to the **design gate** — where I'll propose the concrete persona panel (the ~8 personas above, refined), the file layout for both deliverables, the caching/synthesis format, and the provenance rules — for your approval before any tasks.

Do you want to **approve R18 as-is**, **edit it**, or talk through any AC first? And should I **commit R18** now or hold it in the working tree until the design is also ready?

content: We're adding a spec-driven, multi-agent collaboration retrospective case study to the course. I've drafted requirement R18 and am waiting at the requirements gate for your review. Next: you approve or edit R18, and say whether to commit it. (disable recaps in /config)

I like the requirements but would like to expand R18.AC1 considerably, or into multiple requirements. content.text: Good refinement — that's the right instinct. The grade should be a scannable *summary*, but the substance should be verbose, insight-driven prose. I'll **keep it as one requirement R18** (splitting would fragment a single coherent capability, and ~10 ACs still matches R16/R17 density) but **expand AC1 into three ACs** that separate *scope*, *evaluation form (verbose insights)*, and *the summary grade*, then renumber the rest.

- R18.AC5 — Every reviewer SHALL render **subjective, candid judgments** regardless of its persona: it SHALL NOT flatter, soften to please, or discourage, and SHALL aim for accuracy and thoroughness. Each insight SHALL be **grounded in cited transcript evidence** (session + locator) and SHALL NOT be fabricated or asserted from memory (consistent with R10.AC9 and the R12.AC3 verify-don't-assume discipline).
- R18.AC6 — The evaluation SHALL proceed in **three tiers**: (a) a **per-session × per-reviewer** evaluation that considers only that one session, persisted ("cached") as a committed artifact; (b) a **per-session synthesis** consolidating every reviewer's evaluation of that session; (c) a **global synthesis** across all sessions. Each higher tier SHALL be derived from — and traceable to — the tier beneath it, and SHALL itself carry both the verbose cross-cutting insights (R18.AC2) and the summary grades (R18.AC3).
- R18.AC7 — The retrospective SHALL **attribute the AI side accurately, per session**, to the model that actually produced it (verified from the transcript record, never assumed — e.g., the foundational requirements/design session ran on `claude-sonnet-4-6` while the remaining sessions ran on `claude-opus-4-8`), so that no finding misattributes behavior to a model that did not produce it (R10.AC9 honesty; R12.AC3 philosophy).
- R18.AC8 — The retrospective SHALL be delivered as **both** a learner-facing **case study** (in `course/`, cross-referenced as capstone-exemplar material per R8.AC2 and R14.AC4) **and** a first-class internal **evaluation corpus** (the cached per-session × per-reviewer artifacts plus the per-session and global syntheses), each maintained as a real, accurate deliverable.
- R18.AC9 — The build of this retrospective SHALL itself follow the **spec-driven, gated process** the course teaches (R3.AC2) via the post-v1 enhancement playbook (R13.AC3), and be referenced as a dogfooding example (R14.AC2); the requirement (this R18) SHALL be enforced by the existing dynamically-discovered traceability check (R13.AC5) with **no special-casing**.
- R18.AC10 — The retrospective SHALL keep its references to the source material **accurate to the repository's actual `log/transcripts/` contents** (R14.AC6, enforced by R13.AC4) and SHALL introduce **no new secrets**, relying on the capture workflow's existing secret-scan gate.

lines: **User story:** As a learner (and the course maintainer), I want an honest, multi-perspective retrospective of how this course was actually built with Claude Code — what the human author and Claude each did well, did okay on, and could improve, across both process and human-language communication — produced by a panel of subjective persona reviewers reading the real session transcripts, so that the course teaches effective human+Claude co-authorship from a candid worked example rather than an idealized one. *(Added post-v1, 2026-05-31; anchors additively to R14 (dogfooding), R10 (responsible & verified use), R3 (workflow methods), and R8 (capstone exemplar).)*
**Acceptance criteria**

-- R18.AC1 — THE COURSE SHALL produce a **collaboration retrospective** evaluating its own build, assessing **both parties** — the human author and Claude — along two explicit axes, **process/workflow** and **human-language communication**, with each finding graded on a three-level scale: **did well / did okay / could improve**.

-- R18.AC2 — The retrospective SHALL be produced by a **panel of distinct persona reviewers**, each implemented as a **real, committed Claude Code subagent** (`.claude/agents/`) focused on a key element of effective co-authorship / effective CLI use; the panel SHALL be a genuine dogfooded artifact (R14.AC1–AC3), not a contrived prop, and SHALL be referenced as a worked example from **U13** (subagents) — for which the repo previously had no authentic subagent to show (decision P5-U13-example).

-- R18.AC3 — Every reviewer SHALL render **subjective, candid judgments** regardless of its persona: it SHALL NOT flatter, soften to please, or discourage, and SHALL aim for accuracy and thoroughness. Each judgment SHALL be **grounded in cited transcript evidence** (session + locator) and SHALL NOT be fabricated or asserted from memory (consistent with R10.AC9 and the R12.AC3 verify-don't-assume discipline).

-- R18.AC4 — The evaluation SHALL proceed in **three tiers**: (a) a **per-session × per-reviewer** evaluation that considers only that one session, persisted ("cached") as a committed artifact; (b) a **per-session synthesis** consolidating every reviewer's evaluation of that session; (c) a **global synthesis** across all sessions. Each higher tier SHALL be derived from — and traceable to — the tier beneath it.

-- R18.AC5 — The retrospective SHALL **attribute the AI side accurately, per session**, to the model that actually produced it (verified from the transcript record, never assumed — e.g., the foundational requirements/design session ran on `claude-sonnet-4-6` while the remaining sessions ran on `claude-opus-4-8`), so that no finding misattributes behavior to a model that did not produce it (R10.AC9 honesty; R12.AC3 philosophy).

-- R18.AC6 — The retrospective SHALL be delivered as **both** a learner-facing **case study** (in `course/`, cross-referenced as capstone-exemplar material per R8.AC2 and R14.AC4) **and** a first-class internal **evaluation corpus** (the cached per-session × per-reviewer artifacts plus the per-session and global syntheses), each maintained as a real, accurate deliverable.

-- R18.AC7 — The build of this retrospective SHALL itself follow the **spec-driven, gated process** the course teaches (R3.AC2) via the post-v1 enhancement playbook (R13.AC3), and be referenced as a dogfooding example (R14.AC2); the requirement (this R18) SHALL be enforced by the existing dynamically-discovered traceability check (R13.AC5) with **no special-casing**.

-- R18.AC8 — The retrospective SHALL keep its references to the source material **accurate to the repository's actual `log/transcripts/` contents** (R14.AC6, enforced by R13.AC4) and SHALL introduce **no new secrets**, relying on the capture workflow's existing secret-scan gate.

lastPrompt: I like the requirements but would like to expand R18.AC1 considerably, or into multiple requirements. The evaluations I'm expecting from each agent should not be a selection between three choices, alt…

aiTitle: Build case study with spec-driven development evaluation

content.text:
Done. The key change is splitting old AC1 into three, so the **verbose insight requirement** is now first-class and the grade is explicitly subordinate to it:

> - **R18.AC1** — scope: evaluates **both parties** along two axes (process/workflow, human-language communication).
> - **R18.AC2** *(new — the substance)* — each per-session×per-reviewer evaluation is a **substantive, verbose written assessment, not a selection among fixed categories**. Through its persona's lens it surfaces the session's **key, important insights** — notable moments, recurring patterns, decisive turning points, effective habits, missed opportunities — for both parties, **explained with reasoning** and **ordered by significance**, at whatever length the evidence warrants; covers strengths *and* weaknesses.
> - **R18.AC3** *(new — the grade, now subordinate)* — the three-level grade (**did well / did okay / could improve**) is retained as a scannable **summary verdict** (per party, per axis, and overall) that **summarizes, never replaces,** the AC2 narrative.

And a ripple I folded in: **AC6** (the three tiers) now explicitly says the per-session and global syntheses *also* carry verbose cross-cutting insights plus roll-up grades — so the verbosity requirement propagates up the tiers, not just at the leaf level.

The rest shifted down by the renumber (panel→AC4, candid/evidence→AC5, tiers→AC6, attribution→AC7, both-deliverables→AC8, spec-driven/enforce→AC9, accuracy/secrets→AC10). Nothing references these yet, so the renumber is safe.

I kept it as **one requirement** rather than splitting — AC1–AC3 now read as a clean scope→substance→summary progression, and a separate Rxx would fragment one capability. If you'd rather it be its own requirement, say so and I'll lift AC2/AC3 into an R19.

**Still the requirements gate.** Does the expanded R18 capture what you want — or should I push the verbosity/insight bar further (e.g., require a fixed set of insight categories, a severity scale, or a minimum-depth rubric)? And once you're happy: **approve as-is**, and tell me whether to **commit R18** now or hold it until the design is also ready for review.

message.content: Add a requirement for breadcrumb navigation back to parent documents for all learner-facing documents

content.text: Different concern from R18 (navigation UX, not the retrospective), so it gets its own requirement — **R19**. It anchors naturally to **R15** (accessibility/navigability), **R13** (maintainability + enforcement), and **R6/P7's committed-rendered pattern** (the existing `render-units`/`render-index` tooling). Let me add it after R18.

input.old_string:
- R18.AC10 — The retrospective SHALL keep its references to the source material **accurate to the repository's actual `log/transcripts/` contents** (R14.AC6, enforced by R13.AC4) and SHALL introduce **no new secrets**, relying on the capture workflow's existing secret-scan gate.

---
input.new_string:
- R18.AC10 — The retrospective SHALL keep its references to the source material **accurate to the repository's actual `log/transcripts/` contents** (R14.AC6, enforced by R13.AC4) and SHALL introduce **no new secrets**, relying on the capture workflow's existing secret-scan gate.

### R19 — Breadcrumb navigation for learner-facing documents
**User story:** As a learner moving through the course, I want a breadcrumb trail at the top of every learner-facing document showing where it sits and linking back to its parent document(s) up to the course home, so I never lose my place or have to rely on the browser/editor back button to climb the hierarchy. *(Added post-v1, 2026-05-31; anchors additively to R15 (accessibility & navigability), R13 (maintainability & enforcement), and R6 (unit standard / committed-rendered pattern).)*

**Acceptance criteria**
- R19.AC1 — Every **learner-facing document** SHALL carry a **breadcrumb trail at its top** that links back through its **parent document(s)** up to the course home. In scope: course units and their labs, the capstone briefs/exemplar/rubric and case studies (R18), the CLI reference, the progress checklist, and the "when you're stuck" guide, plus the learner-facing `README`. Out of scope: maintainer-/contributor-facing docs (e.g., the maintainer guide), the spec, and `meta/` artifacts.
- R19.AC2 — Each ancestor breadcrumb segment SHALL be a **working relative link** that resolves to an existing document (enforced by the link check, R13.AC4); the final segment SHALL identify the **current** document and need not be a link.
- R19.AC3 — Breadcrumbs SHALL reflect the document's **actual place in the course hierarchy** (consistent with the course navigation index, R15), and their **placement and format SHALL be consistent across all in-scope documents** and recorded in `meta/conventions.md` so new documents adopt the same pattern (R13.AC1).
- R19.AC4 — Breadcrumbs SHALL be authored in **CommonMark** and degrade gracefully (R15.AC2): the trail SHALL be readable as plain text/links in any Markdown viewer, and its meaning SHALL NOT depend on color, emoji, or non-semantic formatting alone (R15.AC6).
- R19.AC5 — Breadcrumbs SHALL be **enforced mechanically** by the check suite (R13.AC4): a check SHALL fail on a missing, stale, or hierarchy-mismatched trail rather than let it rot silently. WHERE a document is generated (e.g., units' `unit.src.md`→`unit.md` via `tools/render-units`, R6/P7), the breadcrumb SHALL be **single-sourced and emitted by that generator** so it refreshes automatically (R12.AC8 quarantine philosophy).

lastPrompt: Add a requirement for breadcrumb navigation back to parent documents for all learner-facing documents

aiTitle: Build case study with spec-driven development evaluation

content.text:
**R19 added** (working tree only). Summary for the requirements gate:

> ### R19 — Breadcrumb navigation for learner-facing documents
> Every learner-facing doc gets a top-of-page breadcrumb trail linking up through its parents to the course home.
>
> - **AC1** — scope: units + labs, capstone briefs/exemplar/rubric/case-studies, CLI reference, progress checklist, stuck guide, learner `README`. **Out of scope:** maintainer guide, spec, `meta/`.
> - **AC2** — ancestor segments are **working relative links** that resolve (link check); the current segment isn't a link.
> - **AC3** — reflects the **actual hierarchy**, consistent placement/format recorded in `meta/conventions.md`.
> - **AC4** — **CommonMark**, degrades gracefully, no meaning by color/emoji/formatting alone (R15).
> - **AC5** — **enforced mechanically** (a check fails on missing/stale/mismatched trails); **single-sourced via the generator** where a doc is generated (`unit.src.md`→`unit.md`).

Two design-deferred points I want to flag rather than bake into the requirement:
- **The hierarchy itself** (what each doc's "parent" is — e.g., unit → units overview → README) is a design decision; AC3 just requires it be *real and consistent*. P7 already shipped `tools/render-index` for navigation, so breadcrumbs will compose with that.
- **The enforcement mechanism** — likely a new `tools/check-breadcrumbs` (or folding into `check-links`) plus emission from `render-units` — is a design/task call; AC5 only pins the *outcome* (generated where possible, drift-gated).

---

Where things stand at the **requirements gate** — two new requirements in the working tree, uncommitted:
- **R18** — collaboration retrospective (multi-agent self-evaluation), with the expanded verbose-insight ACs.
- **R19** — breadcrumb navigation.

They're independent capabilities, so in the design/tasks phases they'd be separate sections and likely separate task files (e.g., `P9-collaboration-retrospective.md`, `P10-breadcrumbs.md`), though both can ride the same feature branch.

Please review R18 + R19. When you're ready, tell me: **approve both / edit / discuss** — and whether to **commit now** or **hold until design is also ready**. Any more requirements to add before we close the requirements gate?

message.content: approved both R18 and R19. Commit and push.

  on both the human author and Claude, across process and communication;
  three tiers (per-session x reviewer -> per-session synthesis -> global),
  delivered as both a learner-facing case study and an internal corpus.
  Accurate per-session model attribution (sonnet-4-6 for the foundational
  session, opus-4-8 for the rest). Authentic dogfood (real .claude/agents/).

- R19 — breadcrumb navigation: every learner-facing document carries a
  top-of-page trail linking back to its parents up to the course home;
  links resolve, CommonMark, generated where the doc is generated, and
  drift-gated by the check suite.

Design + tasks to follow per the post-v1 enhancement playbook. IDs are
append-only; no can-do taxonomy change. make check green (R18/R19 PEND
until referenced by delivered artifacts).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF

content.text:
Done. ✅

- **Branch:** `feat/retro-and-breadcrumbs` (created off `main`, not committed straight to the default branch)
- **Commit `51d27fe`** — R18 + R19 requirements, full pre-commit check suite green
- **Pushed** — remote `origin/feat/retro-and-breadcrumbs` matches local `51d27fe`; upstream tracking set

The **requirements gate is closed** for both. Per the post-v1 playbook, the next gate is **design** — and I have two independent requirements to serve:

1. **R18 (collaboration retrospective)** — I'll propose, for your approval: the concrete persona panel (the ~8 personas, refined), the three-tier file layout for both deliverables (`course/case-studies/` + `log/evaluations/`), the per-evaluation format (verbose insights + summary grades + evidence citations), provenance/honesty rules, and the per-session model-attribution mechanism.
2. **R19 (breadcrumbs)** — the actual parent hierarchy, breadcrumb placement/format convention, and the generate-and-check enforcement design.

I'd suggest tackling **R18's design first** (it's the larger, more decision-heavy one and the thing you're eager to start running over the transcripts), then R19's. Want me to draft the **R18 design section** for the design gate now, do **both** R18 + R19 design together, or start with **R19** since it's smaller? And these are spec/design edits, so I'll hold them in the working tree for your review before committing — same gate discipline as the requirements.

message.content: I don't plan to do anything more with R19 until R18 is finished. Change the branch name to only reflect the R18 chagnes, clean up the old branch we're currently on, then move to the design phase of R18.

content.text:
On reflection that's the right call — R18 and R19 are independent, and R19 shouldn't drag along R18's branch. Note: both requirements were *approved* and are correctly committed in `requirements.md` (R19 just sits as an approved-but-not-yet-built requirement, showing PEND — that's fine and expected). So I'll only rename the branch to reflect that R18 is the active work; I won't strip the approved R19 requirement out of the commit.

Renaming the branch, cleaning up the old remote, and pulling design context in parallel.

1	<!-- GENERATED from unit.src.md by tools/render-units — do not hand-edit. Edit unit.src.md, then run `make render`. -->
2	---
3	id: U13
4	title: "Delegate a scoped task to a subagent"
5	stage: autonomy-scale
6	depth_tier: core
7	use_case: "Delegate focused / parallel sub-tasks"
8	can_do: [C14]
9	workflows: []
10	coverage_areas: [19]
11	prerequisites: [U12]
12	reading_time_min: 12
13	lab_time_min: 20
14	---
15	
16	[Claude Code Mastery](../../../README.md) › [Course units](../README.md)
17	
18	# Delegate a scoped task to a subagent
19	
20	## Learning objectives
21	
22	By the end of this unit you can:
23	
24	- **Hand a self-contained task to a subagent and use the result it returns** — a separate Claude
25	  instance, with its own context window, that does the work and reports back a conclusion rather than
26	  its whole transcript.
27	- **Recognize when delegation pays off** — a context-heavy exploration you don't want polluting your
28	  main thread, two independent tasks worth running in parallel, or work worth fencing to a restricted
29	  toolset.
30	- **Scope a task so a subagent can finish it without you** — a crisp, self-contained brief with a
31	  defined deliverable, distinct from a routine you reuse in your own context (a skill, [Commands & skills](../12-commands-and-skills/unit.md)).
32	- **Verify what comes back** — a subagent returns a conclusion you didn't watch it derive, so you
33	  check the result against the code instead of trusting that an agent ran.
34	
35	## Fast path (TL;DR)
36	
37	> A **subagent** is a second Claude the main session spins up to handle one scoped task. It has its
38	> **own context window** and can be given its **own restricted tools** — it does the work and returns a
39	> *result* (a summary, an answer, a diff), not its entire transcript. You delegate for three reasons:
40	> **context isolation** (its 30-file search stays in *its* context, only the conclusion lands in
41	> yours), **parallelism** (independent tasks run at once), and **fencing** (a read-only explorer can't
42	> write). Define a custom agent inline with `--agents <json>` or as a file under `.claude/agents/`,
43	> each with a `description` that tells the main agent when to reach for it. The
44	> catch: delegation is *trust delegation* — **verify the result**, don't merge a diff or quote a claim
45	> you didn't check. The lab has you fence a read-only explorer, delegate a real mapping task on
46	> `taskflow-api`, and spot-check what it returns.
47	
48	## Skip-check
49	
50	**Skip this unit if you can already:** hand a self-contained task to a subagent — a separate Claude
51	instance with its own context window and (optionally) a restricted toolset — choosing to do so because
52	the work is context-heavy, parallelizable, or worth fencing; scope the brief so it finishes without
53	back-and-forth; and verify the returned result against the code rather than trusting that the agent
54	ran. If that's already habit, skim the pitfalls and move on.
55	
56	## Concept
57	
58	[Commands & skills](../12-commands-and-skills/unit.md) packaged a routine so you stop re-typing it. A subagent is the
59	next move in the Autonomy stage: instead of *reusing a prompt in your own context*, you **hand a whole
60	task to a separate Claude and use what it gives back**.
61	
62	**1 — What a subagent is.** When the main session delegates, it starts a *fresh* Claude instance with
63	its **own context window**, its own instructions, and (optionally) its own restricted set of tools and
64	permissions. That instance works the task on its own and returns a **result** — a summary, an answer, a
65	patch — to the main session. The key word is *separate*: the subagent's work happens in its context,
66	not yours, and only the conclusion crosses back. You are not watching it think; you are receiving its
67	report.
68	
69	**2 — Why delegate.** Three distinct payoffs, and you should be able to name which one you're after:
70	
71	- **Context isolation.** Answering "where is ownership enforced across this API?" might mean reading
72	  thirty files. Do that in your main session and those thirty files are now wedged in your context
73	  window, crowding out the actual work. Delegate it and the reading happens in the subagent's context —
74	  only the *answer* (file:line list + a sentence) returns. This is the highest-value reason in a long
75	  session: it keeps your main thread clean.
76	- **Parallelism.** Independent scoped tasks can run at the same time — map three subsystems at once
77	  rather than one after another — which is wall-clock faster.
78	- **Fencing (blast radius).** A subagent can be handed a *narrower* toolset than your session has: a
79	  read-only explorer that literally cannot edit or run destructive commands. That's the
80	  [Operate safely](../03-operate-safely/unit.md) blast-radius discipline applied to delegation — give the helper
81	  only the tools its task needs.
82	
83	**3 — How it works in Claude Code.** The main agent invokes a subagent through its task/agent tool;
84	you rarely call it by hand, you *describe a task* and let the main agent dispatch it. You can define
85	**custom** agents two ways: inline for a session with `--agents <json>` (the `--help` example is
86	`{"reviewer": {"description": "Reviews ..."}}`), or persistently as a file under `.claude/agents/`. An
87	agent definition carries a **`description`** — *when* the main agent should reach for it — and usually
88	an allowed-tools list and a model. The `description` is the trigger, exactly as it was for a skill in
89	[Commands & skills](../12-commands-and-skills/unit.md): vague description, never dispatched at the right moment;
90	sharp description naming the situation, picked up when it fits. Background/dispatched agents are managed
91	with the `agents` subcommand. The version-specific surface is the flags, the subcommand, and the on-disk
92	format: `--agent <agent>` / `--agents <json>` set session agents; the `agents` subcommand manages background agents.
93	
94	**4 — Subagent vs. skill.** Both package work; they differ in *where the work runs*:
95	
96	- A **skill/command** ([Commands & skills](../12-commands-and-skills/unit.md)) expands into **your** context — it's a
97	  reusable prompt or capability you run in the session you're in.
98	- A **subagent** runs the work in a **separate** context and hands back a result.
99	
100	So reach for a subagent when the task is **context-heavy** (lots of reading you don't want in your main
101	thread), **parallelizable** (independent of what you're doing), or **worth fencing** (should run with
102	fewer tools than you hold). Reach for a skill when you just want to reuse a prompt right here. A useful
103	tell: if you mostly care about the *answer* and not the steps, delegate; if you want the work done
104	*in front of you* in the current thread, don't.
105	
106	**5 — Delegation is trust delegation.** The subagent returns a conclusion you didn't watch it reach.
107	That makes verification non-optional: a returned diff gets **read** before it's kept; a returned claim
108	("ownership is checked in all five mutation routes") gets **spot-checked** against the code. "An agent
109	ran and reported success" is not verification — it's the thing you verify. This is the same
110	verify-don't-trust habit from [Operate safely](../03-operate-safely/unit.md), and it's the bridge to the trust
111	question you'll face again when you delegate to *third-party* code (MCP servers) later in this stage.
112	
113	**Version currency.** Verified against Claude Code 2.1.159. The inline `--agents`
114	flag, the `--agent` selector, and the `agents` subcommand are `--help`-verified; the persistent
115	on-disk location (e.g. `.claude/agents/<name>.md`) and its front-matter fields are a filesystem
116	**convention** — confirm the exact path and format against `claude --help` and the docs before relying
117	on a detail. `--agent <agent>` / `--agents <json>` set session agents; the `agents` subcommand manages background agents. Tracked in [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md).
118	
119	## Worked example
120	
121	Here is a delegation worth making on `taskflow-api`: *"Across the whole codebase, where is a request's
122	ownership of a project or task actually enforced? Give me the file:line of each check and one line on
123	what it guards."* That's a genuinely context-heavy question — the honest way to answer it is to read
124	the services, the routes, and the dependency wiring. You do **not** want all of that reading sitting in
125	your main context if your real job today is adding a feature.
126	
127	So you fence a read-only explorer and hand it the task. Inline, that's the verified `--agents` shape:
128	
129	```json
130	{
131	  "explorer": {
132	    "description": "Read-only codebase mapper. Use to answer 'where/how is X done across the code?' without polluting the main context. Returns file:line citations + a one-line note each; never edits.",
133	    "tools": ["Read", "Grep", "Glob"]
134	  }
135	}
136	```
137	
138	The same agent kept around for reuse lives as a file under `.claude/agents/explorer.md` (the on-disk
139	**convention** — confirm the path/front-matter against the docs), with that
140	`description` as its front matter and any standing instructions in the body.
141	
142	Two things make this a *good* delegation, and they map straight onto the concept:
143	
144	- **The toolset is fenced to the task.** `explorer` gets `Read`/`Grep`/`Glob` and nothing that writes.
145	  Even if the task drifted, it *cannot* change the codebase — blast radius designed to zero
146	  ([Operate safely](../03-operate-safely/unit.md)).
147	- **The brief is self-contained with a defined deliverable.** "file:line of each ownership check + one
148	  line each" is a crisp finish line. The subagent doesn't need to come back and ask you anything; it
149	  can run to completion on its own. That's the difference between a task you can delegate and one you
150	  can't.
151	
152	What returns is a short report — say, `app/services/projects.py:41 get_project enforces owner_id`,
153	`app/services/tasks.py:33 …`, and so on — costing your main context a dozen lines instead of a dozen
154	files. **Then you verify**: open *two* of the cited lines and confirm the check is really there and
155	really guards what the note claims. You're not re-reading everything — you're spot-checking the
156	conclusion you were handed, because a confident summary of code that isn't there is exactly the failure
157	mode delegation can hide.
158	
159	(The built-in agent types your CLI ships — a read-only explorer, a planning agent — are the same idea
160	pre-packaged; defining your own, as above, is how you fence and brief one for *your* task. Confirm what
161	ships with `claude --help`.)
162	
163	## Lab
164	
165	> **This lab has no automated verifier.** The artifact you produce — a subagent definition — lives in
166	> your own `.claude/`, and whether a delegation is *well-scoped* is a judgment call, not a diff.
167	> So the self-check is an objective checklist you apply to what you built and to the
168	> result you got back, with the worked example's `explorer` as the reference pattern.
169	
170	**Goal:** define a **scoped, read-only subagent**, delegate a real context-heavy task to it against
171	`taskflow-api`, use the result it returns, and **verify that result** against the code — so you can say
172	*why* the work belonged in a subagent rather than inline or in a skill.
173	
174	**Starting state:** the clean primary codebase. `cd codebases/primary/taskflow-api`; create your agent
175	definition under that project's `.claude/` (e.g. `.claude/agents/explorer.md`) so it's scoped to the
176	project. Confirm the path and front-matter format against `claude --help`/the docs rather than
177	authoring it from memory.
178	
179	**Steps:**
180	
181	1. **Define the agent.** Write a read-only explorer: a `description` that states *when* to use it
182	   (specific enough that the main agent would pick it for a "where/how is X done?" question) and a
183	   toolset restricted to reading/searching — no edit or run-destructive tools. Lean on the worked
184	   example as your template.
185	2. **Pick a context-heavy task and write the brief.** Something whose honest answer means reading
186	   several files — e.g. *"List every place pagination/sorting is applied to a list endpoint:
187	   file:line + the parameter that controls it,"* or *"Map the auth flow from login to a protected
188	   route: each hop, file:line."* Make the deliverable crisp so the subagent can finish without asking
189	   you anything.
190	3. **Delegate it** and let the subagent run in its own context. Note what comes back: a short
191	   result, not the files it read.
192	4. **Verify the result (the required step).** Open **at least two** of the cited locations and confirm
193	   each claim holds — the line exists and does what the report says. If a citation is wrong or vague,
194	   that's the finding; the report was a *lead*, not a verdict.
195	5. **(Optional stretch — parallelism.)** Give the explorer two *independent* briefs at once (e.g. map
196	   pagination **and** map the auth flow) and notice they can proceed in parallel because neither needs
197	   the other's output. Dependent tasks can't — that's the boundary of "parallelizable."
198	
199	**Self-check (objective checklist):** you're done when **all** hold:
200	
201	- [ ] The agent is defined with a `description` that states **when** to use it (a situation the main
202	      agent could match), and a **read-only toolset** appropriate to the task — it cannot edit or run
203	      destructive commands.
204	- [ ] The task you delegated was **self-contained with a defined deliverable** — the subagent finished
205	      without needing to ask you a follow-up.
206	- [ ] You **used the returned result** (the conclusion came back to your main session; you didn't have
207	      to re-do the work yourself).
208	- [ ] You **verified the result** — opened ≥2 cited locations and confirmed each claim against the
209	      code, treating the report as a lead rather than trusting it on faith.
210	- [ ] You can **articulate why this was a subagent** — context-heavy, parallelizable, and/or worth
211	      fencing — rather than a skill (reuse a prompt in your own context) or just doing it inline.
212	- [ ] Nothing in the definition **hardcodes a version-specific format from memory** — the path and
213	      front-matter were confirmed against `claude --help`/docs.
214	
215	**Reference:** there's no `solution/` branch — the agent is *yours*. The worked example's `explorer`
216	definition above is the reference pattern; compare your definition and your verification step against it
217	and the checklist.
218	
219	## Common pitfalls
220	
221	- **Delegating work you need to watch.** If the *reasoning* matters to you — a subtle design call, a
222	  risky change — running it in a subagent hides exactly what you wanted to see. Delegate when you care
223	  about the *result*; keep it inline when you need to follow the steps.
224	- **A vague agent description.** Same failure as a vague skill ([Commands & skills](../12-commands-and-skills/unit.md)):
225	  "helps explore code" never gets dispatched at the right moment. Name the *situation* the agent is for
226	  so the main agent can match it.
227	- **Trusting the result because an agent ran.** "It came back green / it said it's done" is not
228	  verification — read the returned diff, spot-check the returned claim. A confident summary of code
229	  that isn't there is the delegation-specific way to get burned.
230	- **An unscoped brief.** A task that needs back-and-forth ("explore the code and let me know what you
231	  find, then we'll decide") can't be delegated cleanly — the subagent stalls or guesses. Give it a
232	  finish line it can reach alone.
233	- **Over-broad tools / permissions.** Handing a subagent edit or bypass permissions it doesn't need
234	  widens blast radius for no reason ([Operate safely](../03-operate-safely/unit.md)). Fence it to the tools its
235	  task requires — read-only for an explorer.
236	- **"Parallelizing" dependent tasks.** Two subagents only run in parallel if neither needs the other's
237	  output. If task B consumes task A's result, it isn't parallel — it's a sequence.
238	- **Authoring the agent format from memory.** The flags, the subcommand, and the on-disk path/front
239	  matter are the version-specific surface — confirm against `claude --help` and the docs rather than
240	  trusting recall.
241	
242	## Going deeper
243	
244	- **Next in the Autonomy stage:** **Hooks** automates *enforcement* — and this repo's own `make check`
245	  suite is its worked example; **MCP & vetting** extends today's trust-delegation lesson to
246	  *third-party* tools you connect and must vet before relying on; **Automate & scale** runs delegated
247	  work headlessly and across parallel worktrees.
248	- **Subagent vs. skill/command** — the form distinction is the [Commands & skills](../12-commands-and-skills/unit.md)
249	  line: reuse a prompt in your own context (skill/command) vs. run a task in a separate context and use
250	  its result (subagent).
251	- **Fencing & verifying delegation** builds directly on [Operate safely](../03-operate-safely/unit.md) (blast
252	  radius, verify-don't-trust).
253	- The flags, `agents` subcommand, and on-disk format: `--agent <agent>` / `--agents <json>` set session agents; the `agents` subcommand manages background agents. Version-specifics in
254	  [`meta/version-record.md`](../../../meta/version-record.md). Confirm with `claude --help`.
255	- Stuck? [`course/stuck.md`](../../stuck.md) and the
256	  [progress checklist](../../progress-checklist.md).

stage: autonomy-scale
depth_tier: core
use_case: "Delegate focused / parallel sub-tasks"
